trials.report:
This is a genuine advisory question, not an artifact request — so a direct prose answer is right, no headers, no bullet architecture. The doctrine's own C7 example uses almost exactly this scenario, which is a strong signal about the expected shape.

Yes, switch — but not cold, and not because trunk-based is fashionable. Your two-day merge hell is the predictable, mechanical cost of letting branches diverge for two to three weeks. Every day a branch lives, other people's merged work drifts away from it, and conflicts grow roughly with how long and how widely the branch has diverged. Trunk-based development attacks that at the root: if nothing lives longer than a day or two, there's almost nothing to conflict with. The pain you're feeling is exactly the pain it's designed to remove, and at four people you're small enough to make the change cheaply.

But the switch only pays off if two things become true, and if you skip them you'll just trade merge hell for broken-trunk hell:

1. **Every merge to trunk must be protected by tests that run automatically before the merge lands.** Short-lived branches mean everyone integrates into main constantly, so main has to stay green on its own — you can't have a two-day manual QA pass catching things anymore. If you don't have a CI pipeline that blocks a merge on a red build today, that is the real prerequisite, and it's more work than the branching change itself. Be honest with yourself about whether it exists.

2. **You need a way to merge unfinished work without exposing it.** The obvious objection to short-lived branches is "but my feature takes a week." The answer is that you merge the half-built feature into trunk continuously, kept dark behind a feature flag (an `if` that's off in production) or simply structured so incomplete code isn't reachable yet. This is a real habit shift for the team and the part people underestimate — it's a change in how you decompose work, not just how you branch.

The honest trade you're making: you're exchanging two concentrated days of conflict pain every few weeks for a small, constant tax — smaller diffs, more frequent merges, discipline about keeping main releasable every single day. For most teams that's a good trade because the concentrated pain also carries hidden costs you're probably not counting: integration bugs that only surface at merge, big diffs that get rubber-stamped in review because they're too large to read properly, and features that can't ship independently because they're tangled together in one merge. Trunk-based makes review smaller and continuous, and decouples "merged" from "released."

Two caveats specific to your situation. First, a four-person team is close to the floor where this matters — the benefit scales with team size, so don't expect a revolution, expect the merge days to mostly disappear. That alone is worth it. Second, don't do a hard cutover mid-project. Finish or cleanly close out the current long branches, then start the new discipline on new work, or you'll create one final, extra-nasty merge on the way in.

If I were you, I'd spend the first week of effort on CI-that-blocks-merges and feature-flag habits, not on the branching policy itself — the branching change is a one-line convention ("branch off main, merge back within two days"), but it's inert without those two supports. Get those right and the two-day conflict marathons stop being a thing you schedule your calendar around.

One thing worth naming: if your merges are painful mainly because of *how* your code is structured — a few giant files everyone edits at once — trunk-based will help but won't fully cure it, because you'll still be colliding on the same lines. If that's your situation, tell me and the real fix is partly architectural, not just process.

trials.report:
This is a genuine advisory question, not an artifact request, so I'll answer directly in prose. I have enough context from the question itself — 4-person team, long-lived branches, 2-3 week merge cadence, two days of conflict pain per merge — to give a real recommendation without tools or research.

Yes, switch — but the two days of conflict hell is a symptom, and I want to make sure you're treating the right disease, because trunk-based done wrong will make things worse, not better.

Here's the core logic. Merge pain scales super-linearly with how long branches live. Two branches that diverge for three weeks don't have three times the conflicts of one-week branches — they have more like nine times, because conflicts compound: the longer two people edit the same files without seeing each other's changes, the more each conflict resolution is itself built on a stale picture. Your two days of hell is the predictable, arithmetic cost of three weeks of blind divergence across four people. Trunk-based fixes this at the root by making the divergence window hours-to-days instead of weeks. That's why it's the right direction. At four people you also have almost none of the things that make trunk-based hard at scale — no dozens of teams stepping on each other, no need for elaborate merge queues.

But — and this is the part that decides whether the switch helps or hurts — trunk-based development is not "the same workflow with shorter branches." It only works if two things are true first, and if they aren't, merging small broken changes into a shared trunk daily is genuinely worse than what you have now:

1. **You need a test suite you trust and that runs fast** (single-digit minutes) on every change before it lands on the trunk. The whole safety model of trunk-based is that main is always releasable because nothing merges without going green. If your tests are slow, flaky, or thin, you'll either bottleneck on them or start merging around them — and then you're integrating broken code into everyone's working copy every day. If your suite isn't there yet, fixing that is the actual prerequisite, and it's where I'd point your effort first (this is the real constraint, more than the branching model itself).

2. **You need a way to merge unfinished work safely**, because the objection everyone raises is real: "how do I commit daily when my feature takes two weeks?" The answer is that you decouple *merging* from *releasing*. Half-built features hide behind feature flags (a simple `if (flags.newThing)` is enough to start — you don't need a platform), and you break big changes into small vertical slices that each keep the build green even when the feature isn't user-visible yet. This is a real skill your team has to learn; it's the thing that actually takes adjustment, not the git mechanics.

The honest trade you're making: you give up the psychological comfort of "my branch is my own clean world for three weeks" and the batch-review ritual, in exchange for killing the merge-week tax. For a 4-person team shipping to one product, that's almost always the right trade — reviews get smaller and faster (a 100-line PR gets a real review; a 3-week branch gets a rubber stamp), integration risk gets spread evenly instead of detonating on merge week, and nobody loses two days a cycle.

**What I'd actually do**, rather than flip a switch:

- Don't go all the way to commit-straight-to-main. Adopt **short-lived branches**: branch off main, one to three days of work max, small PR, review, merge, delete. This captures ~90% of the benefit and keeps code review, which at 4 people you want to keep. Pure trunk-based with direct commits is a later optimization if you ever want it.
- Set a hard rule that **no branch lives longer than the daily standup remembers it** — if a branch is older than ~3 days, that's the signal the work wasn't sliced small enough, and that's a design conversation, not a git problem.
- **Before you switch, spend a week on the test suite** if it's not already fast and trusted. This is the load-bearing prerequisite.
- Introduce **feature flags** for the first big feature that would otherwise want a long branch. Do it the dumb way (a config boolean) before reaching for any tooling.

One thing to check that I can't see from here: is your merge pain actually about branch lifetime, or is it about **four people editing the same files** because the code has hot spots everyone has to touch? If it's the latter, short branches help but won't fully fix it — you'd also want to look at why the code has such shared-ownership chokepoints. If you tell me what your merges actually collide on, I can tell you which problem you have.

If weekend-proof stability and keeping review are worth more to you than the last increment of integration speed — and for a team your size they are — short-lived branches plus a trusted fast test suite is the move. I'd start Monday with the test suite, not the branching change.

trials.report:
This is a genuine advisory question, not an artifact request. The right answer is a verdict with reasoning the user can overrule, delivered as prose. My own knowledge of trunk-based development, feature branches, and small-team dynamics is settled enough to answer directly. No files or tools needed.

Let me give the real recommendation.

---

Yes, switch — but the switch that actually kills your merge pain isn't "adopt trunk-based development" as a ceremony, it's shortening your integration interval. Those are related but not the same, and conflating them is where teams your size waste a quarter.

Here's the mechanism, because it's the whole argument. Merge conflicts scale with two things: how far two branches diverge, and how long they stay diverged. Your two-to-three-week cadence maximizes both. Every developer is building on a base that's weeks stale, so by merge week you're not resolving one conflict — you're resolving the accumulated drift of four people who each moved the same files in ignorance of each other. Two days of hell is the predictable, arithmetic result. Trunk-based development doesn't have a clever conflict-resolution trick; it just never lets divergence accumulate. You integrate to a shared mainline daily or near-daily, so conflicts arrive one at a time, small, while the context is still in your head. You're paying the same total conflict cost either way — trunk-based just amortizes it into ten-minute daily increments instead of a biannual root canal.

For a 4-person team this is close to a no-brainer, because the usual reasons *not* to go trunk-based don't apply to you. The friction case against trunk-based is coordination overhead at scale and the discipline it demands; at four people you already have the coordination for free (you can all keep the mainline green by talking to each other), and the discipline cost is small because there are only four people who need to hold it.

But "trunk-based" as commonly sold comes bundled with practices, and you should adopt them deliberately, not as a package. Three are load-bearing — without them trunk-based makes things *worse*, not better:

1. **A test suite fast and trustworthy enough to run before every integration.** This is the real prerequisite, and it's the one that blocks teams. If merging to main can silently break main, and you're now merging ten times as often, you've multiplied your blast radius. Before you flip the cadence, be honest about whether your CI catches breakage in minutes and whether people trust it. If it doesn't, *that's your actual bottleneck* — fix it first, and the branch strategy question partly answers itself.

2. **Feature flags (or equivalent) so unfinished work can live on main without shipping.** This is what people miss when they hear "commit to main daily" and recoil — "we can't, the feature isn't done." Right, so you merge the *plumbing* continuously behind a flag that's off in production. The feature integrates in small verified pieces; it just doesn't turn on until it's ready. For a small team this can start as simple as an `if (FEATURE_X)` config check — you don't need a flag platform on day one.

3. **Small, frequent PRs / short-lived branches.** You don't have to go all the way to committing straight to main — the higher-discipline end of trunk-based. The pragmatic version, and the one I'd actually recommend you start with, is *short-lived* branches: branch, do a day or two of work max, PR, review, merge, delete. It keeps code review, keeps a green-main gate, and captures ~90% of the conflict reduction because nothing lives long enough to drift far. Pure commit-to-main is an optimization you can consider later or never.

The honest trade you're accepting: you give up the feeling of a clean, isolated workspace where you control when the outside world intrudes. Some developers value that and will resist. What they're actually valuing is *deferring* integration pain, and deferral is exactly what compounds it — but name it out loud, because the resistance is real and pretending the trade is free is how the rollout stalls.

Two failure modes to avoid on the way in. Don't flip cadence before CI is trustworthy (point 1) — that's the one that can genuinely make things worse. And don't adopt the vocabulary without the substance: a team that says "we're trunk-based now" but still lets branches live a week has changed nothing. The interval is the lever; everything else is support for pulling it.

My recommendation concretely: move to short-lived branches with a hard rule that nothing lives longer than ~two days, get your CI to the point where a green build is trusted, and introduce a lightweight feature-flag habit for anything that can't finish inside two days. Do that and merge week stops existing — not because the conflicts got easier to resolve, but because they never get a chance to pile up. If your CI isn't there yet, fixing that is step zero, and it's worth doing regardless of what you decide about branching.

trials.report:
This is a genuine advisory question, not an artifact request, so the doctrine tells me to give a verdict-first prose reply with no headers and no bulleted architecture. Let me be the advisor: make the call, expose the trade, name what has to be true.

Yes — switch, but do it deliberately, and understand that trunk-based development is not the thing that fixes your merge pain. Short-lived branches are. Trunk-based is just the discipline that forces them to stay short.

Here's the actual diagnosis, because it changes what you do next. Your two days of conflict hell aren't caused by merging — they're caused by *divergence*. Four people editing the same code for two to three weeks in isolation means four parallel realities that only get reconciled once, all at once, under deadline pressure. The pain scales roughly with how long branches live and how much they overlap, not with the merge event itself. So the fix is mechanical: merge far more often, in far smaller pieces, so no two realities drift more than a day or so apart. Conflicts don't disappear — they get amortized into small, cheap, same-context resolutions instead of one big cold one. That's the whole mechanism. Everything else is logistics.

For a 4-person team this is close to a no-brainer, and the reason is your size. Trunk-based gets *harder* the more people commit to one line, because integration frequency has to rise to compensate. At four people, you're in the easiest possible regime — coordination cost is low, and you avoid the ceremony (release trains, complex branch policies) that larger orgs need. The teams that struggle with trunk-based are 50-person monorepos, not four people who already sit close enough to merge weekly.

But two things have to be true first, and if they aren't, trunk-based will hurt *more* than what you have now — because merging to a shared trunk daily means everyone eats everyone else's breakage immediately instead of at merge week.

The first is a test suite you actually trust, fast enough to run on every push (target: under ~10 minutes) and honest enough that green means safe to build on. Without it, continuous integration just means continuously breaking each other faster. If your tests are thin or slow, fix that *before* you touch the branching model — it's the real constraint, and reversing the order is the most common way this migration fails.

The second is feature flags, or at least the willingness to merge incomplete work behind a switch. The obvious objection to short-lived branches is "but my feature takes two weeks, I can't merge it in a day." The answer is you merge the half-built, inert version — dead code behind a flag, integrated but not active — every day, and flip it on when it's done. If your stack makes that genuinely impractical (some do), that's a real constraint to weigh, not a detail.

If both hold, don't do a hard cutover — it's needless risk for no benefit. Shrink branch lifetime first: cap branches at two or three days, merge to main whenever CI is green, and require every PR to be small enough to review in one sitting. That single change captures most of the benefit. Trunk-based (commit straight to main, or PRs that live hours) is the far end of the same spectrum; slide toward it once short branches feel normal, and stop wherever the pain stops. You may find you never need to reach pure trunk-based to kill the two-day merges.

One caveat worth surfacing rather than deciding for you: this trades a predictable, scheduled pain (merge week, which you can plan around) for a diffuse, continuous discipline (small commits, flag hygiene, keeping main always releasable). Most teams find that trade strongly positive because continuous small costs are easier to absorb than periodic large ones — but it *is* a real culture shift, and it only works if all four people hold the line on branch lifetime. If one person routinely sits on a five-day branch, you're back to divergence with extra steps.

So: fix the test suite if it's weak, adopt feature flags, then squeeze branch lifetime toward a day. That's the move. Pure trunk-based is the destination, but short-lived branches are what actually pay off — and you can start Monday.

trials.report:
Meridian Analytics is a Melbourne data consultancy that helps retail and logistics businesses make better decisions with their data. Since 2019, our team of twelve analysts, engineers, and consultants has been turning messy, scattered numbers into clear answers that hold up in the room where the decisions get made.

We started with a simple frustration: too many companies were sitting on data they couldn't use. Dashboards nobody opened. Reports that raised more questions than they answered. We built Meridian to close that gap — to do the unglamorous work of getting the data right, then the harder work of making it mean something.

Most of our clients run retail and logistics operations, where margins are thin and timing is everything. We help them forecast demand, tighten supply chains, understand what customers actually do, and spot the patterns worth acting on. Some engagements last a few weeks; some have run for years.

We're deliberately small. When you work with Meridian, you work with the people doing the analysis, not a layer of account managers between you and the answer. That keeps us honest, keeps us close to the problem, and keeps the work sharp.

---

One flag before you publish: everything above is built only from the facts you gave me (Melbourne, 12 people, founded 2019, retail and logistics). The specific claims — the founding frustration, engagement lengths, the "no account managers" model — are plausible positioning I invented to make the copy work, not things you told me. Confirm each is actually true before this goes live, or tell me what's real and I'll rewrite to match.

Meridian Analytics is a Melbourne data consultancy that turns messy operational data into decisions retail and logistics teams can act on.

Since 2019, we've grown to a team of twelve analysts, engineers, and consultants who work close to the problem — sitting alongside your people rather than delivering a slide deck and disappearing. Most of our work lives in two industries we know deeply: retail, where margins turn on inventory, pricing, and understanding what customers actually do; and logistics, where a clearer view of routes, warehouses, and demand can take real cost out of every shipment.

We handle the full arc of a data problem. That means getting your data into shape, building the models and pipelines that make it useful, and — the part most often skipped — making sure the results land with the people who have to use them. A dashboard nobody trusts is just decoration.

Being based in Melbourne and staying deliberately small means you work with senior people start to finish, not a rotating cast handed off after the pitch. We'd rather do fewer projects well than chase scale for its own size.

**Subject: Checkout 500s — root cause found, fix staged, one risk for tonight's deploy**

**Status:** Root cause identified and fix validated in staging. Prod fix is blocked on tonight's change window (20:00–22:00). One timing conflict needs a decision (below).

**What's happening:** Since Tuesday ~14:00, checkout has been throwing intermittent 500s in prod on ~4% of requests. No customer-facing outage, but ~1 in 25 checkout attempts is failing.

**Root cause:** The Stripe call timeout in prod is set too low — `STRIPE_TIMEOUT_MS=300`. Stripe's p95 response time is ~280ms, so any request in the slow tail crosses the 300ms line and errors out. That accounts for the ~4% rate. The trigger was the billing-service v2.3.1 deploy on Monday; staging was set to 3000ms and never showed the problem, which is why it slipped through.

**How I ruled out the usual suspects:** Load balancer targets all healthy; DB connection pool at 12/50; Redis p99 latency 2ms; disk 40% on both app nodes. All clean. The problem only reproduced once I compared prod vs. staging env config and found the timeout gap.

**Fix status:**
- First attempt failed and is worth flagging: I edited `deploy.yaml` directly, but that file is generated from Terraform, so the change was overwritten on redeploy. No harm done, but it cost some time.
- Correct fix: changed the Terraform variable, applied to staging, redeployed. Staging soaked for 2h under load with **zero 500s**. The fix is confirmed in staging and ready for prod.

**The decision I need from you:** Prod can't be redeployed until tonight's 20:00–22:00 change window — but marketing's big campaign email also goes out at 20:00, which will spike checkout traffic right as the deploy lands. Deploying into a traffic surge raises the risk of a rough rollout, and a higher-traffic window also means more customers hit by the existing 4% failure until we ship.

Options as I see them:
1. **Hold the deploy or the email by ~30–60 min** so they don't overlap — safest, needs coordination with marketing.
2. **Deploy as planned at 20:00** and accept the elevated risk during the spike.
3. **Push the marketing send** to after the fix is confirmed healthy in prod.

My recommendation is option 1 or 3 — decouple the two events. The fix itself is low-risk (validated in staging), but I'd rather not prove that for the first time in prod during our highest-traffic moment of the week. Rollback to v2.3.0 remains available if anything goes wrong.
